items.title: They contacted you first (unsolicited)
items.description: Most scams begin with unsolicited contact — a text, email, call, or social media message you didn't expect. Legitimate companies rarely cold-contact individuals with urgent requests.

items.title: Message from unknown number or email
items.description: Scam messages often come from unknown numbers, unfamiliar email addresses, or addresses that closely mimic real companies (e.g., support@amaz0n.com).

items.title: Claims to be from a bank, government agency, or tech company
items.description: Scammers impersonate trusted organizations (IRS, banks, Microsoft, etc.) to create urgency and authority. Real agencies send official letters first and never demand immediate payment by phone or text.

items.title: Uses generic greeting ('Dear Sir/Madam', 'Dear Customer')
items.description: Mass-distributed scam messages use generic greetings because they're sent to thousands of people. Legitimate communications from your bank or service providers use your name.

items.title: Contacted you on an unexpected platform
items.description: Receiving a message about your bank account on WhatsApp, or a job offer via Instagram DM, is unusual. Legitimate organizations communicate through their official channels.

items.title: Creates urgency ('act now', 'limited time', 'expires today')
items.description: Scammers create artificial urgency to prevent you from thinking clearly, researching, or consulting others. Legitimate offers and communications give you time to decide.

items.title: Threatens account suspension or closure
items.description: Claims your account will be suspended, locked, or deleted unless you act immediately. Real companies don't threaten immediate account closure via text or unsolicited calls.

items.title: Threatens arrest, lawsuit, or legal action
items.description: Scammers threaten arrest warrants, lawsuits, or criminal charges to create fear. Real legal proceedings are served formally — law enforcement does not call to demand payment.

items.title: Tells you not to tell anyone or consult others
items.description: Scammers isolate you from people who might recognize the scam. They say 'don't discuss this with anyone' or 'this is confidential.' Legitimate transactions don't require secrecy.

items.title: Uses emotional manipulation (guilt, fear, sympathy)
items.description: Appeals to emotion — sick children, dying relatives, desperate circumstances — to bypass your rational judgment. Scammers weaponize your empathy.

items.title: Insists you stay on the phone while making payment
items.description: Scammers keep you on the line to prevent you from hanging up and verifying their claims. This is a hallmark of tech support scams and impersonation scams.

items.title: Requires upfront payment to receive something
items.description: You must pay a fee, tax, or charge before receiving a prize, inheritance, loan, or job. Legitimate prizes never require payment, and employers never charge you to start a job.

items.title: Asks for payment via gift cards
items.description: No legitimate business, government agency, or organization accepts payment via gift cards. This is one of the strongest scam indicators — gift cards are untraceable and irreversible.

items.title: Asks for payment in cryptocurrency
items.description: Demands for crypto payment are a major red flag — crypto transactions are irreversible and nearly impossible to trace. Legitimate businesses offer standard payment methods.

items.title: Asks for wire transfer or money order
items.description: Wire transfers (Western Union, MoneyGram) cannot be reversed once sent. Scammers prefer these because the money is gone immediately with no way to recover it.

items.title: Insists on unusual payment method
items.description: Won't accept credit cards or standard bank transfers, insisting instead on payment apps, prepaid debit cards, or other methods that are hard to reverse or trace.

items.title: Payment requests are escalating
items.description: Started with a small amount and now they want more — additional fees, taxes, or 'complications' that require further payment. This pattern never ends.

items.title: Sent you too much money and wants some back
items.description: Overpayment scams send fake checks or 'accidental' payments, then ask you to return the difference. The original payment bounces and you lose the money you sent.

items.title: Promises guaranteed investment returns
items.description: No legitimate investment guarantees returns. Promises of guaranteed profits, risk-free investing, or unrealistic percentages (especially per day/week) are hallmarks of fraud.

items.title: Offer seems too good to be true
items.description: Free money, amazing deals, incredible returns, or winning a contest you never entered. If it seems too good to be true, it almost certainly is.

items.title: Asks for personal information (SSN, DOB, etc.)
items.description: Requests for your Social Security number, date of birth, mother's maiden name, or other personal details — especially via text, email, or unsolicited calls — are attempts at identity theft.

items.title: Asks for bank login or account details
items.description: No legitimate company or bank employee will ever ask for your online banking password, full account number, or PIN via text, email, or phone call.

items.title: Asks you to share a verification code
items.description: They ask for a code sent to your phone. Sharing a 2FA or verification code gives scammers access to your accounts. No legitimate service asks you to share these codes.

items.title: Wants copies of ID documents
items.description: Requests for photos of your driver's license, passport, or other ID documents are attempts to steal your identity. Legitimate verification happens through secure, official portals.

items.title: Wants remote access to your computer or phone
items.description: Being asked to download TeamViewer, AnyDesk, or other remote access software gives scammers complete control of your device. Tech support scammers use this to steal data and money.

items.title: Contains links to click
items.description: Suspicious messages contain links to phishing sites that look like real login pages. Always go to websites by typing the URL yourself — never click links in unexpected messages.

items.title: Cannot verify through official channels
items.description: You can't find the company, person, or organization through independent research. Legitimate entities have verifiable online presence, contact information, and registrations.

items.title: Caller ID or email looks official but feels off
items.description: Scammers can spoof phone numbers and create email addresses that look nearly identical to real ones. Don't trust caller ID or sender addresses alone — verify independently.

items.title: No written contract or official documentation
items.description: Legitimate business deals, jobs, and investments come with written agreements. If they resist putting anything in writing, it's a red flag.

items.title: Website looks unprofessional or recently created
items.description: Scam websites are often new, have poor design, missing contact info, or copied content. Check the domain registration date and look for reviews before trusting a site.

items.title: No independent reviews or references
items.description: A legitimate company or investment platform will have independent reviews, media mentions, and a track record. If you can only find information provided by them, be suspicious.

items.title: Claims to have a secret or insider method
items.description: There are no secret methods to guaranteed wealth. Claims of insider information, secret algorithms, or exclusive access are designed to make you feel special and bypass your skepticism.

items.title: Shows screenshots of their own profits
items.description: Scammers show fake screenshots of trading profits to build trust. These are easily fabricated. Never invest based on someone else's claimed returns.

items.title: Uses an unfamiliar trading or investment platform
items.description: Fake platforms look professional but aren't registered with financial regulators (SEC, FINRA). Always verify any investment platform through official regulatory databases.

items.title: Cannot withdraw your money from the platform
items.description: The platform shows profits but you can't withdraw — they demand fees, taxes, or larger deposits first. This is the most obvious sign you're on a fraudulent platform.

items.title: Encouraged to recruit friends or family
items.description: Being asked to bring in others is a hallmark of pyramid and Ponzi schemes. Legitimate investments don't need recruitment — they stand on their own merits.

items.title: Must pay for training, equipment, or background check
items.description: Legitimate employers never charge you to start a job. Fees for training materials, equipment, or background checks that you must pay upfront are scam indicators.

items.title: Job sounds too easy for the pay offered
items.description: Promises of high pay for minimal work (data entry, product reviews, social media likes) are designed to lure you in before extracting money or using you as a money mule.

items.title: Hired without a real interview
items.description: Being offered a job without a proper interview, especially through text or messaging apps, is a red flag. Real employers have structured hiring processes.

items.title: Asked to receive and forward packages or money
items.description: Being asked to receive packages and reship them, or receive money and forward it, makes you an unwitting accomplice in criminal activity (money laundering or trafficking stolen goods).

items.title: Asked to use your personal bank account for business
items.description: A 'employer' who wants to deposit funds into your personal account and have you transfer them is using you as a money mule. This is illegal and you will be held responsible.
